trip.name: West Highland Way
trip.description: The West Highland Way is Scotland's oldest and most celebrated long-distance trail, stretching 154 km from the lowland town of Milngavie on the outskirts of Glasgow to Fort William at the foot of Ben Nevis. Over seven walking days, the route passes through an extraordinary progression of landscapes — from gentle farmland and ancient woodland, along the storied shores of Loch Lomond, across the vast wilderness of Rannoch Moor, past the dramatic mountains of Glencoe, and over the famous Devil's Staircase before descending to Fort William. Opened in 1980, it was Scotland's first officially designated long-distance route and remains by far the most popular, drawing over 50,000 walkers each year. With a group of 15 friends and luggage transfer arranged, this is the perfect way to experience the trail — walking light by day, gathering in Highland inns by evening.

days.label: Milngavie to Drymen
days.description:
A gentle introduction to the West Highland Way, starting from the granite obelisk in Milngavie town centre. The trail follows the Allander Water before winding through the ancient woodland of Mugdock Country Park, where moss-covered trees filter the light. You'll pass the tranquil Craigallian Loch and the cottages of Carbeth before the landscape opens out into rolling farmland beneath the Campsie Fells.

The walking is straightforward throughout — good paths, no technical terrain, and only modest undulations. After passing the distinctive volcanic plug of Dumgoyne, which rises dramatically above Glengoyne Distillery (well worth a short detour for a dram), the trail follows an old railway line and quiet lanes before descending through fields into Drymen.

Drymen is a charming village with a good range of pubs, restaurants, and a SPAR shop — your last reliable resupply point before Tyndrum on Day 4. The Clachan Inn claims to be the oldest licensed pub in Scotland. Make the most of the village atmosphere tonight; the wild Highlands are calling from tomorrow.

accommodation.name: Winnock Hotel / Buchanan Arms / Drymen Inn

days.resupply_notes: SPAR shop in Drymen, open daily. Last major resupply before Tyndrum (Day 4). Stock up on snacks and any essentials.
water_sources: Water tap at Beech Tree Inn (halfway)
escape_routes: Bus from Drymen to Glasgow (First Bus 309, roughly hourly service)

waypoints.name: Milngavie Obelisk (Start)
waypoints.note: Official start of the West Highland Way — cafes, shops, ATMs nearby

waypoints.name: Mugdock Country Park
waypoints.note: Ancient woodland, visitor centre with cafe and toilets

waypoints.name: Craigallian Loch
waypoints.note: Scenic loch with memorial to the Craigallian Fire — a symbol of Scottish outdoor access

waypoints.name: Carbeth
waypoints.note: Water tap by trail. Distinctive hutting community

waypoints.name: Beech Tree Inn
waypoints.note: Food and water tap — good lunch stop

waypoints.name: Dumgoyne viewpoint
waypoints.note: Views of the volcanic plug above Glengoyne Distillery. Short detour for whisky tasting

waypoints.name: Drymen village
waypoints.note: Village is 1 km off the main trail. SPAR shop, pubs, restaurants

days.label: Drymen to Rowardennan
days.description:
Today delivers the first genuine highlight of the Way: the ascent of Conic Hill (361m). After passing through Garadhban Forest, the trail climbs steadily to a summit that sits directly on the Highland Boundary Fault — the geological dividing line between the Scottish Lowlands and the Highlands. From the top, the panorama over Loch Lomond and its scattered islands is breathtaking, and you can literally see the change in landscape from the gentle south to the rugged north.

The descent from Conic Hill into Balmaha is steep and rocky in places — take care, especially if wet. Balmaha is a bustling little village on the loch shore with the Oak Tree Inn providing excellent food and a welcome rest stop. From Balmaha, the trail follows the eastern shore of Loch Lomond through mixed woodland, passing beautiful pebble beaches and oak-fringed bays with views across the water to Ben Lomond (974m).

This is a longer day at 24 km with meaningful ascent over Conic Hill and constant ups and downs along the lochside. The terrain is generally good but includes some rocky and rooty sections toward Rowardennan. Accommodation in Rowardennan is limited — the Rowardennan Hotel and SYHA Youth Hostel are the main options, plus the Oak Tree Inn back in Balmaha.

accommodation.name: Rowardennan Hotel / Oak Tree Inn Balmaha

days.resupply_notes: Small shop in Balmaha (seasonal hours). No shops in Rowardennan. Stock up in Drymen before leaving.
escape_routes: No road access from Rowardennan — nearest bus is Balmaha or arrange taxi pickup
warnings: Steep descent from Conic Hill — take care in wet conditions
Wild camping ban in effect along Loch Lomond shore (March-September) — use designated sites only

waypoints.name: Drymen turn-off (trail junction)
waypoints.note: Rejoin main trail from Drymen village

waypoints.name: Garadhban Forest
waypoints.note: Forest track, gentle climbing begins

waypoints.name: Conic Hill summit (361m)
waypoints.note: Highland Boundary Fault. Spectacular panorama over Loch Lomond and islands

waypoints.name: Balmaha
waypoints.note: Oak Tree Inn for food. Public toilets. Small shop (seasonal). Last services before Rowardennan

waypoints.name: Craigie Fort viewpoint
waypoints.note: Lochside viewpoint with bench

waypoints.name: Sallochy Bay
waypoints.note: Beautiful pebble beach — good rest spot

waypoints.name: Rowardennan
waypoints.note: End point. Hotel, youth hostel. Ben Lomond trailhead nearby

days.label: Rowardennan to Inverarnan
days.description:
This is widely regarded as the toughest day on the West Highland Way — not for the distance or ascent, but for the relentless terrain along the eastern shore of Loch Lomond. The path is a constant obstacle course of exposed tree roots, slippery rocks, muddy hollows, and scrambles over boulders, demanding continuous concentration on your footing. Allow significantly more time than the distance alone would suggest.

The first half to Inversnaid is the most demanding section. The trail clings to the steep, wooded lochside, with frequent ups and downs that are exhausting in aggregate. At Inversnaid, you'll find the historic Inversnaid Hotel (seasonal) and the dramatic Inversnaid Falls — a waterfall immortalised by Gerard Manley Hopkins. The second half from Inversnaid to Inverarnan follows the River Falloch through increasingly Highland scenery, with the terrain gradually improving.

Despite the difficulty, this is one of the most beautiful stages. The ancient oak woodland, the lapping waters of the loch, and the sense of isolation create an unforgettable atmosphere. Arrive at Inverarnan grateful for the Drovers Inn — a wonderfully eccentric 300-year-old Highland inn with stuffed animals on the walls and live music on many evenings.
days.resupply_notes: No shops between Rowardennan and Inverarnan. Carry all food and water for the day. Drovers Inn serves meals. Small shop at Ardlui marina (seasonal).
water_sources: Beinglas Farm campsite water tap near Inverarnan
escape_routes: Inversnaid: ferry to Inveruglas on the west shore (seasonal, check timetable)
Ardlui: train station on the West Highland Line (Glasgow-Fort William service)
No road access between Rowardennan and Inversnaid — no mid-stage bailout on the first half
warnings: Most technically demanding day — tree roots, wet rocks, and scrambling throughout
Allow extra time: terrain slows pace significantly despite modest distance
Very limited mobile signal along the loch shore
Carry sufficient food and water — no services for 14 km until Inversnaid

waypoints.name: Rowardennan
waypoints.note: Start from hotel/hostel

waypoints.name: Ptarmigan Lodge
waypoints.note: Trail becomes rougher beyond this point. End of camping management zone

waypoints.name: Rowchoish Bothy
waypoints.note: Open shelter, basic refuge in emergency

waypoints.name: Inversnaid Falls
waypoints.note: Dramatic waterfall. Hotel (seasonal) with bar meals. Ferry to Inveruglas

waypoints.name: Rob Roy's Cave
waypoints.note: Rocky overhang reputedly used by Rob Roy MacGregor

waypoints.name: Doune Bothy
waypoints.note: Open shelter maintained by the Mountain Bothies Association

waypoints.name: Beinglas Farm
waypoints.note: Campsite with water tap and basic shop. Falls of Falloch nearby

waypoints.name: Inverarnan / Drovers Inn
waypoints.note: Historic 300-year-old inn. Bar meals, live music. Ardlui station 1 km south

days.label: Inverarnan to Tyndrum
days.description:
A welcome change of pace after yesterday's lochside scramble. The trail follows Glen Falloch northward through increasingly Highland scenery, with the River Falloch crashing through rocky gorges and over waterfalls beside the path. The terrain is far better underfoot — mostly good tracks and paths through open glen and forested hillside.

The route climbs gradually through the glen, passing the Falls of Falloch (a worthwhile short detour) before reaching Crianlarich, a small Highlands village just off the main trail. Crianlarich has a train station, a small shop, and cafes — a useful resupply point. Beyond Crianlarich, the trail follows old military roads and farm tracks through Strath Fillan, passing the atmospheric ruins of St Fillan's Priory, before reaching Tyndrum.

Tyndrum is a welcome oasis with two hotels, a bunkhouse, the famous Green Welly Stop, a fish and chip van, and the Real Food Cafe. There's a Co-op for supplies — your last proper shop before Fort William. The village sits in a spectacular bowl of mountains and marks the transition into the true Highland wilderness ahead.
days.resupply_notes: Small shop in Crianlarich (off-route, 1.5 km detour). Co-op in Tyndrum — last shop before Fort William. Green Welly Stop for outdoor gear and snacks. Real Food Cafe for excellent fish and chips.
escape_routes: Crianlarich: train station on the West Highland Line
Tyndrum: Citylink bus to Glasgow or Fort William (multiple daily)
Tyndrum Lower station: trains to Glasgow

waypoints.name: Inverarnan
waypoints.note: Start from Drovers Inn

waypoints.name: Falls of Falloch
waypoints.note: Short detour to dramatic waterfall on the River Falloch

waypoints.name: Derrydaroch
waypoints.note: Cattle farm, trail follows river

waypoints.name: Crianlarich turn-off
waypoints.note: 1.5 km detour to Crianlarich village — shop, cafes, train station

waypoints.name: Kirkton Farm / St Fillan's Priory
waypoints.note: Ruins of medieval priory in Strath Fillan. Atmospheric spot

waypoints.name: Tyndrum
waypoints.note: Green Welly Stop, Co-op, Real Food Cafe. Last shop before Fort William

days.label: Tyndrum to Kingshouse
days.description:
The longest day on the Way, but arguably the most spectacular. The route traverses the full grandeur of the central Highlands, from the wooded flanks of Ben Dorain to the vast, treeless expanse of Rannoch Moor, before arriving at the iconic Kingshouse Hotel beneath the towering pyramid of Buachaille Etive Mòr.

The first section climbs alongside the West Highland Railway to Bridge of Orchy, with the sweeping flanks of Ben Dorain (1076m) dominating the view. From Bridge of Orchy, a short stretch over old drovers' roads leads to the Inveroran Hotel — a historic coaching inn that makes a perfect lunch stop. Beyond Inveroran, the trail crosses the shoulder of the Black Mount before descending onto Rannoch Moor itself. This vast, peat-bog wilderness stretches to the horizon in every direction — one of the last true wilderness areas in Europe. The old military road provides solid footing across the moor.

The day culminates with the approach to Kingshouse, where Buachaille Etive Mòr rises suddenly from the moor like a giant sentinel guarding the entrance to Glencoe. The recently renovated Kingshouse Hotel, in this location since 1750, is the perfect reward. Start early today — this is a long day but the scenery justifies every step.
days.resupply_notes: No shops between Tyndrum and Kingshouse. Inveroran Hotel serves lunch (check seasonal hours). Kingshouse Hotel has a bar and restaurant. Carry all food and snacks for the full day.
water_sources: Streams on Rannoch Moor (plentiful but treat before drinking)
escape_routes: Bridge of Orchy: train station on West Highland Line
Inveroran: limited — road access only, no public transport
Kingshouse: Citylink bus stop on the A82 (Glasgow-Fort William service)
warnings: Longest day at 30.5 km — start early and pace yourself
Rannoch Moor is fully exposed with no shelter — carry waterproofs and warm layers regardless of forecast
Limited mobile signal across Rannoch Moor
No shops or reliable food sources between Tyndrum and Kingshouse — carry a packed lunch and snacks

waypoints.name: Tyndrum
waypoints.note: Start from Tyndrum. Green Welly Stop for last supplies

waypoints.name: Ben Dorain viewpoint
waypoints.note: Sweeping views of Ben Dorain (1076m) across the glen

waypoints.name: Bridge of Orchy
waypoints.note: Village with hotel, station, and bunkhouse. Good rest stop

waypoints.name: Inveroran Hotel
waypoints.note: Historic coaching inn. Lunch stop with bar meals (seasonal hours)

waypoints.name: Black Mount ascent
waypoints.note: Trail climbs onto the moor. Views of Black Mount peaks to the west

waypoints.name: Ba Bridge (Rannoch Moor)
waypoints.note: Crossing point on the vast moor. Stunning 360-degree wilderness views

waypoints.name: Kingshouse Hotel
waypoints.note: Historic inn since 1750. Recently renovated. Bar, restaurant, bunkhouse. Iconic view of Buachaille Etive Mòr

days.label: Kingshouse to Kinlochleven
days.description:
A shorter but dramatic day featuring the most famous climb on the West Highland Way: the Devil's Staircase. Despite its fearsome name, this is a well-graded zigzag path climbing 250 metres to the highest point on the entire trail at 549 metres. The effort is spectacularly rewarded with views south over Glencoe's great ridges, east across Rannoch Moor, and north toward the Mamores and Ben Nevis.

From Kingshouse, the trail follows the old military road to the base of the Staircase. The climb is steady rather than brutal — the zigzags were originally engineered for soldiers and pack horses. At the summit cairn, take time to savour the panorama before beginning the long descent toward Kinlochleven. The path drops through a scenic Highland landscape of heather moorland and scattered birch, with the Mamores ridge line filling the northern horizon.

Kinlochleven is a sizeable town at the head of Loch Leven, with a Co-op, several pubs and restaurants, and the Ice Factor — the world's largest indoor ice climbing facility. After the minimalism of Kingshouse, the town's amenities feel luxurious. Enjoy the shorter day and rest your legs for the final push to Fort William tomorrow.
days.resupply_notes: No food or shops between Kingshouse and Kinlochleven — carry packed lunch. Co-op supermarket in Kinlochleven for final day resupply. Multiple restaurants, cafes, and takeaways in town.
water_sources: Streams on the Devil's Staircase (plentiful, treat before drinking)
escape_routes: No mid-stage bailout — once on the Devil's Staircase, continue to Kinlochleven
Kinlochleven: Stagecoach bus 44 to Fort William and Glencoe village
warnings: Devil's Staircase summit is exposed — wind and rain can make conditions challenging
Long descent (614m) is hard on knees — consider trekking poles
No shelter or services between Kingshouse and Kinlochleven

waypoints.name: Kingshouse Hotel
waypoints.note: Start from hotel. Fill water bottles before leaving

waypoints.name: Altnafeadh (A82 crossing)
waypoints.note: Road crossing. Last chance for Citylink bus bailout. View into Glencoe

waypoints.name: Devil's Staircase base
waypoints.note: Zigzag climb begins. Well-graded military road

waypoints.name: Devil's Staircase summit (549m)
waypoints.note: Highest point on the WHW. Spectacular panorama over Glencoe, Rannoch Moor, and Mamores

waypoints.name: Loch Eilde Mòr viewpoint
waypoints.note: Mountain loch visible below the trail

waypoints.name: Kinlochleven
waypoints.note: Town with Co-op, pubs, restaurants. Ice Factor indoor climbing centre

days.label: Kinlochleven to Fort William
days.description:
The final day is a fitting climax to the West Highland Way. The route climbs out of Kinlochleven through birch woodland into the Lairigmor — a dramatic mountain pass flanked by the Mamores to the east and the Grey Corries to the north. Walking through this pass on the old military road, surrounded by some of the finest mountain scenery in Scotland, is one of the trail's defining moments.

Beyond the pass, the trail descends through forestry plantations before reaching the magnificent Glen Nevis, where the imposing bulk of Ben Nevis (1345m) — the highest mountain in the British Isles — dominates the skyline. The sense of approaching journey's end, with Britain's greatest mountain as the backdrop, is deeply satisfying.

The final stretch follows the glen's access road into Fort William, where the trail ends at a statue of a walker in Gordon Square. This is the moment to celebrate with your group — 154 km of Scotland's finest landscapes conquered together. Fort William has an excellent range of restaurants and pubs for a well-deserved celebration dinner. No accommodation or resupply along the route today, so carry everything you need.
days.resupply_notes: No shops or services between Kinlochleven and Fort William — carry all food and water. Fort William has full town facilities: supermarkets, outdoor shops, restaurants, and pubs.
water_sources: Streams in the Lairigmor (treat before drinking)
escape_routes: No mid-stage bailout — no road access or public transport between Kinlochleven and Glen Nevis
Fort William: train and bus connections to Glasgow, Edinburgh, and Inverness
warnings: No services or shelter for the entire 25 km — carry sufficient food, water, and waterproofs
Lairigmor pass is exposed and can be windy
Final forest section has multiple path junctions — follow waymarks carefully
Start early to allow time for a comfortable finish and celebration

waypoints.name: Kinlochleven
waypoints.note: Final resupply at Co-op. Fill water bottles before leaving

waypoints.name: Lairigmor (pass entrance)
waypoints.note: Old military road enters the mountain pass between Mamores and Grey Corries

waypoints.name: Lairigmor summit
waypoints.note: Highest point of the day. Spectacular mountain scenery

waypoints.name: Blar a' Chaorainn (forest)
waypoints.note: Trail enters forestry plantation. Multiple junctions — follow waymarks

waypoints.name: Nevis Forest viewpoint
waypoints.note: First views of Ben Nevis through the trees

waypoints.name: Glen Nevis
waypoints.note: Emerging into the glen with Ben Nevis towering above

waypoints.name: Fort William (Gordon Square)
waypoints.note: Official end of the West Highland Way! Statue and plaque mark the finish

weather.description: May is one of the best months for walking the West Highland Way. Days are long with up to 17 hours of daylight, temperatures are mild but cool at altitude, and rainfall is typically lower than summer months. However, Scottish weather is notoriously changeable — expect anything from warm sunshine to cold rain and wind within the same day. Highland areas and exposed passes (Rannoch Moor, Devil's Staircase) can feel significantly colder due to wind chill. Midges are generally not yet active in May, which is a significant advantage over June-September.
alerts: Scottish weather changes rapidly — always carry waterproofs and warm layers regardless of forecast
Wind chill on exposed sections (Rannoch Moor, Devil's Staircase, Lairigmor) can drop temperatures significantly
Snow is possible on higher ground in early May, though unlikely mid-month
UV can be strong despite cool temperatures — carry sunscreen
Midges typically not active until late May/early June — carry repellent as a precaution

gear_checklist.item: Broken-in waterproof hiking boots with ankle support
Camp shoes or sandals for evenings
Water bottle or hydration bladder (minimum 1.5 litres)
Sit mat or lightweight sit pad
Dry bags or zip-lock bags for electronics
Fully charged mobile phone with offline maps (e.g., Maps.me, OS Maps app)
Basic first aid kit (blister plasters, painkillers, antiseptic)
Paper map or guidebook as backup
High-energy snacks (trail mix, energy bars, chocolate)
